Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic even though I spent a lot of hours on coursework. I'd often find myself reading through materials late at night when the anxiety hit, going through the same passages multiple times. I tried to organize things but I'd get overwhelmed and just drift between different courses without a clear plan. The mindfulness exercises I started doing helped me focus a bit better toward the end, but I still felt scattered most of the time.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really. Some weeks I'd be up at 2 AM cramming before a deadline, other weeks I'd barely touch anything until panic set in. I wanted to have a regular schedule but between my anxiety and just feeling sad a lot of the time, I couldn't stick to anything consistent. Deadlines were basically the only thing that forced me into action.
Q3: Usually it was either panic about an upcoming deadline or just this restless feeling where I knew I should be doing something productive. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important because the anxiety about falling behind was constant. Other times I'd see a notification and feel obligated to at least look, even if I wasn't in the right headspace to actually engage.
Q4: Yeah, eventually logging in became almost automatic, like checking my phone. I think it happened because I was enrolled in so many online courses that the LMS was just always there in my life. It wasn't necessarily productive routine though—sometimes I'd log in, scroll around, and log out without really accomplishing anything meaningful. The habit formed but the engagement quality varied wildly.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were definitely most useful for me since that's how I learn best. I could download PDFs and go through them at my own pace, highlighting and taking notes. The discussion forums were also helpful when I actually participated because seeing other people's perspectives made concepts click better. Videos were okay but I preferred having text I could re-read.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the optional supplementary materials and some of the peer interaction stuff. When you're taking nineteen courses and struggling with limited resources, you have to prioritize. I'd tell myself I'd come back to the extra readings but I rarely did. Some of the interactive elements felt like busywork when I was already overwhelmed.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to assignments or exams, or if it genuinely helped me understand something I was confused about. I needed to see the immediate value because I couldn't afford to waste time or energy on things that seemed peripheral. If something made a concept clearer or reduced my anxiety about an upcoming assessment, it was worth it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford, honestly. Required ones had this weight to them—the anxiety would build until I completed them. With optional stuff, I'd feel guilty for not doing them but also resentful that they existed because they added to my mental load. It's like required activities were non-negotiable stressors while optional ones were just additional sources of guilt.
Q9: Oh absolutely, most of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download everything I could and work from printed notes or PDFs on my tablet because I couldn't always rely on stable internet. I have notebooks full of handwritten summaries and diagrams that the LMS would never know about. A lot of my 31 hours of studying happened away from any digital tracking.
Q10: There were whole weekends where I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes, re-reading downloaded materials, or just thinking through concepts while doing other things. I'd sometimes study while commuting or waiting around, using materials I'd saved. The LMS would show nothing for those days but I was definitely learning. My exam scores reflect knowledge that wasn't built entirely online.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture PDF or reading once, and then that document would live on my laptop for weeks while I studied from it repeatedly. My internet isn't always reliable and I have limited data, so downloading once and studying offline just made practical sense. The LMS only saw that one download, not the ten times I reviewed it afterward.
Q12: There were definitely periods where everything felt impossible. When my anxiety spiked, I'd freeze up and avoid the LMS entirely even though that made things worse. Feeling sad made it hard to care about coursework, and with low resources, sometimes basic life stuff took priority over studying. Those weeks my attendance dropped and I'd miss classes I actually wanted to attend.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed by having nineteen courses to juggle, I'd just go through the motions on the LMS without really absorbing anything. Confusion about course material would make me avoid it entirely because facing it felt too hard. Financial stress and other responsibilities meant sometimes the LMS got whatever scraps of attention I had left after dealing with real life.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have materials open, I'd be scrolling through, but my mind would be somewhere else entirely—worrying about other deadlines, feeling sad, just not present. I'd watch videos without processing them or read pages without retaining anything. The LMS would log my time but it wasn't quality engagement at all.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially when they felt overwhelming or when my anxiety was bad. I knew certain assignments were important but facing them meant facing the possibility of failure, which was terrifying. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but tomorrow I'd feel the same. The mindfulness stuff helped me recognize this pattern but breaking it was still really hard.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system, which is exhausting. I'd have these bursts of intense LMS activity right before things were due, cramming and submitting at the last minute. Without a deadline looming, I'd drift and avoid. It meant my engagement looked really uneven—nothing for days then suddenly everything at once.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it gave me a temporary boost and I'd engage more for a little while. But negative feedback or even just uncertainty about how I was doing would trigger my anxiety and make me want to avoid the course entirely. It's complicated because I needed feedback to improve but receiving it was emotionally difficult. The positive feedback on my exam scores helped my mood a lot.
Q18: The structure mattered a lot for someone like me who struggles with self-direction. Courses with clear weekly modules and explicit expectations were easier to follow than ones where I had to figure things out myself. But even good structure couldn't fully compensate for my tendency to drift when things got emotionally hard. I needed the structure but I also needed to be in the right mental state to follow it.
Q19: Having materials available for download made engagement way easier given my internet situation. Clear deadlines helped too, even if they stressed me out. What made it harder was when courses had lots of small tasks spread out—that triggered my anxiety because there were so many things to potentially fail at. Discussion requirements were tough because interacting with peers when you're feeling sad and anxious takes a lot of energy.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on a bad week—logging in repeatedly, clicking around, maybe rewatching things, but being too anxious or overwhelmed to actually complete and submit work. You might be engaging with the material but unable to produce anything because the pressure of submission feels too high. High activity can mask avoidance behaviors or just genuine struggle to convert engagement into output.
Q21: That actually describes me pretty well. My exam score was over 90 but my attendance was only 50% and I know my LMS activity was inconsistent. I studied a lot offline, I processed materials in my own way, and I performed well on assessments despite not looking super engaged online. The LMS doesn't capture the learning that happens in your head or on paper.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, whether you complete things. They're probably good at identifying people who are completely absent. But they miss the quality of engagement, the offline studying, the emotional state behind the clicks. They can't tell if someone scrolling through materials is deeply learning or completely dissociated. They measure presence, not understanding.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with the material, not just going through the motions. It's when you're curious, when concepts stick, when you think about the course content even outside of study time. For me, real engagement happened in moments when my anxiety quieted down enough for genuine learning to occur. It's mental and emotional, not just behavioral.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with anxiety throughout this course, or how the mindfulness intervention actually helped me cope better toward the end. It wouldn't show the hours I spent studying from downloaded materials or handwritten notes. It wouldn't capture that despite feeling sad and disconnected a lot of the time, I genuinely learned and earned my distinction through effort that happened mostly off-screen.
